desc:
A southern path leads towards a cluster of old and decaying ruins nestled in a
grove of trees. To the west a large stone bridge spans the raging waters of the
river Mitheithel and on the other side of the bridge a steep and rocky slope can
be seen. While the foliage looks more lush on this side of the river, signs of
wildlife are scarce.
name: Junction to an Inn

desc:
The road bends sharply towards the south, avoiding a steep slope just east of
here. At the northern edge of the road a sombre country of dark trees winding
among sullen hills are visible, while to the west the road leads towards a large
decaying stone bridge. The river Mitheithel can be seen to the west, flowing
rapidly from its sources far north in the Ettenmoors.
name: Bend in the Old East Road

desc:
A sheer cliff, forming the northern bank, rises sharply from the water and atop
this bluff stands a large tower. The upper levels of the structure are missing,
but it is nevertheless an impressive building. Attacking the fortress from here
would be quite impossible. On the southern shore of the river a light forest
grows.
name: The Mitheithel

desc:
The southern bank of the river is covered by trees but on the eastern bank the
only thing that covers the ground is small sharp rocks. Beyond the sloping
riverbank to the east a forest can be seen. It is difficult to tell what kind of
trees predominate there, but they appear to be very large. The river is
partially blocked by rubble fallen from the ruined stronghold to the north.
name: The Mitheithel

desc:
To the west, well protected behind the river and by a steep cliff, lies an old
ruined fortress. Once four towers marked the corners of this edifice. Now, only
one tower remains and that one only partially intact, the rest having been
reduced to rubble as has much of the fortress wall. Most of the debris has
tumbled into the river leaving massive, angular, grey stone blocks everywhere.
The waterway cuts a narrow path through the valley with its eastern bank covered
by moss and rotting waterlogged trees which appear close to death.
name: The Mitheithel

desc:
Squeezed between the river to the west and an impressively large fortress to the
east, this small island provides little room to manoeuvre. The skill of the
masons who built the fortress was superb for while the fortress looks truly
ancient it will probably stand for many more years. The northern part of the
stronghold is nothing but ruins, apparently destroyed by both siege machines
many years ago and continual erosion from the river.
name: Before the Gate

desc:
Before you stands the last remaining guard tower of this stronghold. The stone
walls of the tower are several feet thick and a bronze door, which still guards
the entrance to the tower, has turned green. The last few floors of the tower
are gone, the tower looking as if a huge sword had sliced off the top.
name: Old Courtyard

desc:
This was once a large and mighty structure built to protect the old, and now
nearly forgotten, Kingdom of Rhudaur. It lies well protected behind a river that
the people of Rhudaur had named the Hoarwell. Before the structure fell into
disrepair this was a powerful fortress that commanded the countryside for many
miles. However, during some long forgotten war the whole northern section of the
fortress was razed and only this courtyard, the gate house and a partly ruined
guard tower remain.
name: Inside the Gate

desc:
East of here, a large building containing stables, barracks and storage rooms
once stood and was probably the last line of defence in the stronghold. However,
the building became a death trap when the northeastern guard tower fell upon it
reducing the building to rubble and the inhabitants to corpses. Not much remains
of the guard towers: while part of the south western tower still stands the
other three towers are nothing more than heaps of stone.
name: Centre of the Old Courtyard

desc:
The southern battlement still stands high above your head. Once long ago a high
tower secured each end of the wall, but the eastern tower is now little more
than a low ring of large stone blocks, while the western tower ends a few feet
above the wall. The courtyard surface is made out of square stones fitted close
together. Everywhere along the courtyard old twigs, pieces of wood and
decomposing leaves litter the ground.
name: Old Courtyard

desc:
To the south a tower once stood in southeastern corner of this stronghold. The
tower was utterly destroyed during the assault that befell this fortress. As the
tower came tumbling down it took parts of the wall with it into the river far
below. To the east and north are large piles of stone that are the remains of a
perimeter wall and a building.
name: Old Courtyard

desc:
An extremely hot fire must have raged here during the assault because the stone
blocks that once formed the towers wall are fused to the floor where they fell.
This tower seems to have collapsed under its own weight and much of it has
fallen south - into the river below. Weeds now grow here and there through the
cracks and along the gaping hole in the floor.
name: Ruined Tower

desc:
These stairs lead up towards the second level of the tower. They were built very
narrow to enhance the defence of the tower. The slits in the wall were made to
allow the archers on the inside of the fortress to rain death upon anyone who
attacked the fortress. The ground is several yards below and a fall from here
would have nasty consequences.
name: Tower Stairwell

desc:
Once this was one of the four towers built to guard the fortress, but now only
this tower still stands. Halfway up the northern side of the tower there are
three large holes causing the tower to look as if something similar to a huge
sledgehammer had repeatedly struck it. To the east a stone staircase begins to
spiral towards the second level.
name: Ruined Tower

desc:
Much of the northern section of the fortress is in ruins. While the northern
wall of the fortress, which was once over 15 feet tall, still stands, nowadays
it is little more than ten feet high. The two northern guard towers have fallen
inwards onto the courtyard and to the west there is a huge pile of grey rubble
obstructing movement in that direction.
name: Old Courtyard

desc:
North of here, the northeastern guard tower used to stand. However, the piles of
rubble you see before you, blocking passage to the north are all that remains of
the tower. The northern wall, which once joined the now ruined tower, has
several holes in it that were created by a large catapult.
name: Old Courtyard

desc:
This room was probably used to store emergency supplies for the guards in the
tower. Remains of swords, almost rusted beyond recognition, lie against one of
the walls. To the east a seemingly long tunnel leads into the darkness.
name: Storage Room

desc:
The Rhudaurian king that ordered the building of this fortress realised that its
position behind the rivers made it possible for his enemies to trap the garrison
inside. So a long tunnel, going east under the river, was dug to ensure that
counterstrikes as well as escape could occur from the stronghold. The tunnel is
rather damp and the walls are covered with moss. To the west it ends in what
seems to be a rectangular room while the eastern end of the tunnel is shrouded
in darkness.
name: Escape Tunnel

desc:
The tunnel is rather narrow here and water trickles in through narrow fissures
in the roof above your head. The rusted remains of old torch-holders line the
walls. The smooth floor slopes upward from here in both directions, creating a
low point, where almost a foot of chilly water has accumulated. The footing is
extremely treacherous, for the combination of water and thick, green moss make
it quite slippery.
name: Damp Tunnel

desc:
The walls and the floor of the passage are black granite, crossed by a few
purple mineral veins. The stonework is reminiscent of dwarven stonework, but the
shapes are lighter and the lines are not so deeply cut. At the eastern end of
the passage a few stone steps lead up into the darkness. The only flaw in the
perfection of this finely-wrought tunnel is a rough-sided hole at the base of
the stairway.
name: A Granite Passage

desc:
The ceiling here is very low, and the black granite walls curve inwards slightly
below it, just short of forming an arched roof or a dome. A few steps lead
downward at the western end of the chamber, and another set of stairs leads up
the northern wall to a strange rusty contraption that hangs from the roof.
name: Granite Chamber

desc:
This fairly straight tunnel seems to have been dug without regard to the kinds
of rock it passed through, but then the softer grey stone must have crumbled
under the pressure of the many tons of rock and collapsed, revealing the base of
black granite underneath. Broken slabs and chunks of rock lie all around you,
almost completely blocking the eastern part of the passage.
name: Collapsed Tunnel

desc:
The tunnel is almost perfectly round in shape as if it had been dug by a
burrowing creature that made no distinction between up, down, left, and right.
The floor merges smoothly with the walls, and the walls arch to form the roof.
Only deep scratches in the rock that look like marks of sharp claws suggest that
maybe the creature preferred to walk or crawl on its paws and did know the
difference between the floor and the ceiling. The tunnel is fairly level, but to
the west it slopes up, entering a wider passage.
name: Sloping Tunnel

desc:
Built to enable troops to move safely between guard towers, this tunnel appears
to have been carved through dense bedrock. In the eastern end of the tunnel
there is a pool of old rainwater that has leaked from a rectangular hole in the
roof above. A massive stone stair leads up through the hole. In the western end
of the tunnel there is a massive metal door.
name: Tunnel

desc:
This is a perfectly circular chamber. The walls are fitted with fine-cut,
interlocking flagstones. A short passageway leads north to a rectangular room
and a stone stairway leads through the ceiling high above you. To the east there
is a metal door.
name: Tower Basement

desc:
Rough rocks and boulders stand tumbled all about you, and the floor is thick
with smaller rubble and debris. The unfinished walls, floor and ceiling of the
tunnel suggest that this place might not have been wrought by the hand of dwarf
or man, but here and there the tell-tale signs of tooling seem to offer the
support an opposing view. What does seem clear is that the massive rock- fall
which closes off the tunnel to the west of here was brought about quite
deliberately.
name: A Broken Passageway

desc:
The first thing that strikes anyone stepping into this chamber is the feeling
that a power older than the power of these hills and also much more violent is
present here. This presence seems to weigh heavily on the senses - the air seems
hard to breathe, and even in the direct light of a lamp or a torch everything
around seems darkened as if seen through dark glass. The ceiling of the chamber
is very high, and in the western wall there is an arched door, which looks as if
it had been made for a giant. A sloping passage leads down from the opening in
the eastern wall.
name: Large Chamber

desc:
The passage slopes up slightly as it runs from the east to the west, where it
seems to end in a level chamber. To the east of you an ornamented door in the
rock marks the entrance into the underground crypt. Far overhead the stone walls
bend slightly, forming an arched ceiling. The passage is about ten feet in width
and even more in height, and one can only wonder what the stone-masons did to
all the rock they had to cut out.
name: Wide Passage

desc:
No outside light penetrates into this corner of the cavern, yet the darkness is
not as complete as one would expect so deep underground. Luminous veins and
specks of minerals in the stone shine with a weak bluish radiance, unnoticeable
in the bright light of a torch or a lantern, yet enough to guide the eyes used
to the dark. The western wall of the cavern seems to have been smoothed out and
polished by the stone-cutters, but no markings of any kind can be seen on its
surface.
name: Underground Crypt

desc:
The cavernous expanse continues to the north, west, and east, its dome-like
ceiling lost in the dark. Even though the panels in the floor conceal graves,
there is nothing morbid about place; instead, there is a feeling of timelessness
and serenity. The dead were brought here and put in the stone to rest, and the
hills have accepted them and forgotten them as only a thing older than any
living being can forget.
name: Underground Crypt

desc:
This is the northern part of an enormous cavernous chamber in the very heart of
the hills. Obviously, it used to be a natural hollow in the rock which was made
into a crypt where the priests of the temple have buried the dead. The
stone-masons did little to adorn the place; the walls and the ceiling seem to
have kept their original shape. Ornamented panels in the floor mark the places
where slabs of stone were cut out to make place for coffins, but no name, date,
or inscription marks the panels. The cavern continues to the south and to the
east; an arch in the northern wall leads into a smaller chamber.
name: Underground Crypt

desc:
The stairwell ends here with a wide arch that opens into a cavern-like chamber.
Judging by the room's present shape, the stone-cutters who carved it were either
unable to cut too deep into the stone, or afraid to disturb the weight of many
tons of rock above, and so contented themselves with chipping off sharp edges
and corners and smoothing the floor rather than trying to give the room a
finished form. An arch in the southern wall of the room leads into a larger
chamber.
name: A Chamber in the Rock

desc:
It is very dark and quiet here, as if the darkness was muffling all sounds. This
part of the cavern has not been touched by the stone-cutters; the walls, the
floor, and the ceiling have kept their natural shape. Ancient beyond all human
memory yet untouched by decay, the place looks unreal, half-familiar, and
somewhat sad like a picture from a long forgotten dream.
name: Underground Crypt

desc:
This part of the cavern is darker than the rest, but there is an unmistakable
draught of fresh air coming from the shaft that is cut in the ceiling of the
cavern. A narrow cast-iron stair winds around an iron pole and disappears in the
darkness of the shaft. The iron steps were crafted to look like lace, yet they
can obviously support great weight.
name: Underground Crypt

desc:
The wide stairs of the spiral stairwell lead down into the dark. Carved from the
bone of the hills, the grey stone walls are plain, not decorated by anything
except an occasional coloured vein. In some places the strength of the stone
defeated the instruments of the stone-cutters, and they were forced to give up,
leaving the walls or the steps slightly uneven.
name: Winding Stairwell

desc:
A winding staircase leads up and down from the landing. About ten steps below
you the walls and the stairs merge with the darkness, as if the stairwell was
ending with a drop into a bottomless pit. Some light and a draught of air comes
from the above.
name: On a Landing

desc:
The hidden passage runs from the east to the west. The walls are covered with
pictures of burial ceremonies and of other less peaceful rituals, carved in
stone with merciless precision. At the eastern end of the passage you can see a
heavy dark curtain, covering the entrance to some room. To the west the passage
seems to end in a landing of a stairwell.
name: The Hidden Passage

desc:
The high ceiling of the small dark study is lost in the gloom. A heavy marble
desk in the centre of the room is littered with books and scrolls, some of them
quite old and fragile-looking. One wall of the study is lined by bookshelves,
the other is covered by a dark curtain. The third wall is occupied by a
fireplace in which the embers of fire glow with a dim red light. No lamp or
candle lights the room, the owner of which is obviously used to working with no
more light than the glow of the coals on the fireplace.
name: The Priest's Study

desc:
There is a heavy trapdoor in the ceiling of the narrow shaft in the rock. From
the trapdoor a winding cast-iron stair descends the shaft into the darkness.
Just next to you there is an iron door set in the rock wall; a faint draught of
air comes from behind it.
name: Narrow Winding Stair

desc:
The ceiling and the walls of the chamber are plain grey stone except for two
bands of some unfamiliar bluish alloy which are fixed to the walls where the
walls join the floor and the ceiling. The floor is covered with square panels,
each of them engraved with the same ornament of a square with chipped corners
cut into four parts. In the western wall of the chamber there is an arch that
opens into a dark hall.
name: Small Chamber

desc:
Gloom seems to fill the air of this dark hallway which stretches north-south
ending with a low arch. A finely crafted lamp hangs over the arch, shining with
a cold white-blue radiance, but its light is not bright enough to dispel the
gloom in the hall or to penetrate the darkness beyond the arch.
name: Dark Hallway

desc:
It is very dark here, and there is a draught of cold air coming from the south
where the hallway is choked by debris. Obviously, a wall and a part of the
ceiling had once collapsed here, blocking passage in that direction. Some of the
chunks of stone look like pieces of ornamented panels; a few are curved as if
they had once been parts of an arched structure.
name: Dark Hallway

desc:
The four windows look out in the four directions on the gloomy landscape.
Overhead you can see two crossed beams on which the heavy bell once hung, rung
in praise, mourning or just out of boredom. Somebody cut the thick rope, and the
bell now lies uselessly on the ground. The sides of the bell are grey, but
inside it is still shiny and seemingly untouched by weather and time, as if it
were only resting and waiting for the day when it would ring again.
name: In the Bell-tower

desc:
This corner of the battlement has suffered heavy damage from the elements over
the years. With the abandonment of the fortress, there was no one was left to
oversee its maintenance. After every heavy rain, water would collect on and
between the boards of the wooden floor as the rain water ran from the tower to
the west into this corner. Slowly the floor began to rot, leaving it in a state
in which it could barely support the weight of a small child much less the
heavier mass of a grown adult.
name: Decaying Battlements

desc:
Just to the west of here, in a narrow canyon, the river Mitheithel flows rapidly
towards the south. On the other side of the river a large but ruined fortress
can be seen. It appears difficult to get to the fortress from here because a
wide river with steep banks blocks direct access to the fortress.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
A vast rocky plain spreads towards the north, broken only by the narrow valley
with the Mitheithel to the north-west and a dark and brooding forest to the
north-east. The grass that grows here is coarse and grey and the few trees that
cling to life are bent and misshapen. The ground is covered with small sharp
rocks and slopes toward the river. On the far side of the river, atop a high
rocky mound, a ruined tower can be found. It appears that the tower was once
well fortified and long ago might have been nearly impossible to take by force
of arms.
name: Light Forest

desc:
The floor of this dense forest slants downwards towards the north-west where the
river Mitheithel flows while due north the forest becomes more sparse. The land
here is wild and pathless making travel difficult even though the forest thins
to the north. To the south lies the edge of a deep ravine. The sides of the
ravine are steep and while it would be easy to slip into the ravine, it will be
much more difficult to climb out.
name: Edge of the Forest

desc:
The trees stand densely packed all the way to the southern bank of the river
Mitheithel. The ground is covered with a soft mat of brown needles that were
underwater not so long ago. On the other side of the river a ruined tower can be
seen against the horizon. It is in ruins and a huge heap of stone blocks at the
base of the remaining tower suggests that it was once a lot higher.
name: Edge of the Forest

desc:
Bounded on two sides by the river, a grove of trees fills this region making it
a safe haven during floods. To the north the river Mitheithel joins with one of
the tributaries that flowed from the highlands, and together they create a large
fork in the water. In the middle of that fork, at the northern edge of the
joining rivers, a ruined tower, upon a cliff, can be seen against the backdrop
of the horizon.
name: Light Forest

desc:
These fields, squeezed between the river Mitheithel to the west and the dark
forests to the north and east, form a narrow valley. The rough ground is
composed of rocks of many different sizes and shapes. Close to the edge of the
river, bushes grow in dense patches with large barren spaces between them. To
the south the ground gradually becomes more level and an old road leads towards
a large stone bridge in the south-west. On the other side of the road the ruins
of a number of small decaying buildings can be seen, standing as a clump in a
copse of large trees.
name: Fields

desc:
The land among the hills is pathless and travel is encumbered by fallen trees
and tumbled rocks. The tops of the high trees are swaying slightly in the wind
and the ground is covered with patches of heather underlain by a soft mat of
brown needles. Further northward the forest looks denser and darker as the signs
of wildlife in that direction become sparse.
name: Forested Hills

desc:
The ravine winds its way though a sombre country of dark trees. The sides of the
ravine are steep and the footing uncertain. To the south an old and obviously
decaying road can be seen at the foot of the sullen hills. The smell is heavy
and musty, probably because of rotting vegetation.
name: Forested Ravine

desc:
The shallow ravine slowly leaves the Old East Road behind as it starts to twist
its way northward towards the Ettendales far north of Rivendell. The western
slope of the ravine is rather steep and it is littered with rocks of all sizes
and shapes. Further north the hills around the ravine rise higher and higher and
the forest that covers those hills looks dense and dark.
name: Shallow Ravine

desc:
At this location the Old East Road sweeps northward to avoid a cluster of large
and decaying buildings that lies in the grove of trees to the west. Not much
remains of the buildings - one is partly burnt down and another looks more like
a pile of rocks then the building it once was. On the southern side of the road
vast plains that were once farmland spread across the land towards the river
Mitheithel in the south-west.
name: Bend in the Old East Road

desc:
To the north of The Old East Road there is a narrow ravine that leads north-
wards through the steep lands on the north side of the road. From here the road
is several miles east of a narrow valley in which the Mitheithel flows. On the
south side of the road the land is more level and in the distance a dark and
sombre forest can be seen.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The road runs east-west through a dark green forest. To the northwest the forest
is cut by a narrow ravine, which leads northwards through the steep hilly land.
It is quiet here, and the silence is at times so complete, that one can hear
miles away the sound of waters of Mitheithel swirling against the three great
arches of the Last Bridge.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The road leads steadily east-west through the unfriendly country of dark trees
winding among the feet of sullen hills. Here and there upon heights and ridges
you can catch glimpses of ancient walls of stone and the ruins of towers; they
have an ominous look.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The road runs west-south here. Some tracks lead away from the road to the north,
cutting in two the uneven grassy plain and eventually disappearing in the forest
which runs up a hill-slope. Looking in that direction, you can see a tall
building on top of the slope, the lower part of the building almost completely
concealed by the trees.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The pines here are not very old, yet something in their appearance gives them a
tired look, as if they have been standing here for many centuries. A shallow
ravine cuts through the forest to the west. To the south you can see the Old
East Road make its way through the terrain. To the north the forest seems to
become lighter; looking in that direction, one can see spots of bright colours
in the gaps between the trees.
name: Pine Forest

desc:
Not yet hardened by the cold wind and the harsh winters of the Lone-lands, young
green pines grow here on the very edge of the Old East Road. The boles and the
branches of the trees seem to be disproportionately thin, and the fresh green
colour of their needles seems to be too bright for this cheerless landscape.
name: Light Pine Forest

desc:
Neither grassland, nor heath, nor swamp, this odd patch of land looks as if
somebody put it together by mixing pieces of all the three types of landscape.
The two tracks, that cross it from the north from the south, seem to have been
made by one passage of a very heavy carriage with very big wheels. Except for
that, there seems to have been little or no other traffic, yet strangely enough
no vegetation grows on these tracks, and the ground around them seems to be
whitened as if with early frost.
name: On the Old Tracks

desc:
The road bends north-east here passing a hill to the north and a dark forest to
the south. A small stream trickles out of the forest onto the side of the road
and disappears in a ditch. The stream carries a few tree-leaves of an odd shape:
they resemble a clawed paw of an animal.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The Road turns west-south here, following the bends of the feet of the hills.
Some pines grow on the crown of the hill to the north, their tops and branches
sharply outlined against the sky. The hill to the east of you is very high, and
its slope seems impossible to climb.
name: Old East Road

desc:
The slopes of the hill are covered with trees, which climb all the way up from
the forest to the north. The hills to the west are less forested, and the crown
of one of them is completely bare, except for a lonely rock rising in its
middle. The eastern and the southern sides of the hill are almost vertical, and
far below to the south you can see the Old East Road.
name: Forested Hill

desc:
A small mound rises here on the border of a pine forest. The sides and the top
of the mound are covered with heather, and there is an even circle of stones set
on its top like a crown. The heather plants inside the stone circle have white
flowers and a serene look as if, like the flowers of evermind, they were
dedicated to mourning the dead.
name: Heathery Mound

desc:
This is the highest point of the hill ridge and probably the best place to get a
view of the Lone-lands. Far below you can see the Old East Road sweeping round
the feet of the hills, and far to the north you can see another hill ridge
rising dark across the sky. A building rises on the westmost point of the
northern ridge, but at this distance it is hard to make out any details.
name: On the Crest of the Ridge

desc:
From this rocky hill to the north you can see most of the hilly plain overgrown
with trees, grass, moss, and heather. Whoever placed the hills on this plain
must have been following some clear plan at first, but then grew bored and
scattered the rest of his store at random.
name: On the Ridge of Hills

desc:
A lone grey rock rises in the middle of the wide flat crown of the hill. The top
of the rock is carved in the shape of a deep bowl. The inside of the rock-hewn
bowl is blackened as if by fire, but the water in the bowl is as clear as a
crystal, not a blemish marring its transparent depths.
name: The Crown of the Hill

desc:
The hill ridge runs from the west to the east. Far below to the south the Old
East Road holds on its way from the Last Bridge to the River Bruinen. Pine trees
and fallen rocks cover the northern slopes of the ridge, which drop into a
narrow dark vale. The southern slopes are almost bare and descend to the road in
a series of stony shelves.
name: On the Ridge of Hills

desc:
Some decades, maybe some centuries ago, somebody laid a few wooden beams in the
forest soil here to prevent it from sliding down into the narrow valley to the
west. Whoever took care of the forest then is now probably long dead, but the
wooden beams still seem quite solid, even though they are now old and overgrown
with moss. Dark pines and firs rise all around you; if any paths ever passed
through the forest here, they are now concealed by a thick carpet of
pine-needles.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
The valley turns north-west here, growing wider to the north. The floor and the
sides of the valley have a rounded shape, as if somebody had scooped out the
sandy soil of the forest with a giant scoop. Conifers grow on both sides of the
valley, and its floor is covered with dry branches and pine-needles.
name: Deep Valley

desc:
The long valley is narrow, deeply cloven, dark and silent. Trees with old and
twisted roots hang over cliffs and pile up behind into mounting slopes of
pine-wood. The slopes to the north and the south are pathless, encumbered by
fallen trees and tumbled rocks.
name: Narrow Valley

desc:
Two slopes meet here. A rocky slope to the south climbs to the top of a hill
with a wide flat crown, whereas the sandy northern slope rises only about ten
feet upward and then disappears in the forest. To the east the sides of the
depression become steeper and closer to each other, forming a narrow valley.
name: Rocky Depression

desc:
A hill ridge rises just to the south. The level ground continues the steep side
of the ridge. The pattern of rocks, moss, and trees on the ground repeats the
pattern of the slope so accurately, as if one sheet of turf with rocks, trees,
and moss on it was drawn both across the sheer hillside and this level slope.
name: Rocky Grounds

desc:
The hills have now begun to shut you in. The Road behind holds on its way to the
River Bruinen, but both are now hidden from view. A path leads up the slope of
the hill to the south, picking its way among the boulders. Pines follow the
path, marking its turns, but stop just short of the top, as if unwilling to
settle on the highest point of the land.
name: Rocky Grounds

desc:
A few pines grow on the rocky crown of the hill, looking down at the Old East
Road. The slopes of the hill are a mess of rocks, moss and fallen branches. In
the middle of the circle of pines rain water has collected in a slight
depression in the rock. The water is clear and cool, and pine-needles float on
its surface.
name: Rocky Hill

desc:
Rocks have been torn out of the ground here and strewn around, as if this place
had been a playground of the stone giants. The rocks and the ground are powdered
with a white substance which looks like rime or salt. A few rocks are broken in
pieces, as if the inner pressure had made them burst apart; their insides
glitter with small colourless crystals.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
Thick green moss covers the ground here, in places completely concealing pits,
ditches, stones, rocks and small mounds. The moss seems unnaturally thick and
soft, as if somebody put or planted it here on purpose, wishing to hide
something beneath it. To the south a hill ridge rises at some distance; to the
east and to the north you can see a sombre forest.
name: Mossy Slope

desc:
The air is very dry here, and there is an unpleasant smell you cannot recognise.
Some rocks lie scattered on the mossy ground between the trees. Judging by the
shape of the rocks, they had once been partly melted and then allowed to cool in
whatever shapes they assumed. The moss on the ground around the rocks is black,
as if some substance in the soil made it change its colour.
name: Edge of the Forest

desc:
The trees here are mostly oaks, but their foliage and bark are somewhat darker
than normal, and the leaves seem to be of a slightly wrong shape, although when
you look at them closely, you see nothing unnatural. The soil is very dry, and
there is almost no undergrowth. The forest ends just to the south of you, where
the ground slopes down into a rocky depression.
name: Edge of the Forest

desc:
Pines and oaks grow together in this part of the forest, but if you moved in any
direction, you would find that the trees soon separate. The oaks will follow you
to the west or to the north, whereas the pines run down the slopes to the deep
valley in the southeast. The leaves of the oaks seem to be darker than the
pine-needles, but this could be a trick of the uneven lighting of the forest.
name: Mixed Forest

desc:
Here a river once cut its way through the thick soil of the forest, washing away
clay, sand and any vegetation that got in its way. Now the riverbed is dry and
covered with pine-needles, and only the gnarled tree roots that hang from the
cliffs remind of the valley's origin.
name: Deep Valley

desc:
The forest ground slopes into a deep ravine to the west. To the north of you the
way is blocked by a hedge of young fir-trees, their branches hanging so low and
intertwined so closely that no animal larger than a fox could possibly find
there a gap large enough to squeeze through. Spider-webs hang on the tips of the
branches of the trees, a few drops of water on them shimmering like tiny
crystals.
name: Hedge in the Forest

desc:
In this part of the forest for every live pine there are two tree-stumps or
uprooted pines, as if somebody had once set out to destroy the forest
completely, but then gave up the task, being either bored, content, or just no
more in need of pine-wood. To the south the way is blocked by a hedge of young
firs; to the west you can see a small clearing behind the trees.
name: Forest by the Hedge

desc:
A faint trail, little more than a small path, winds its way through the forest.
In places it is choked with fallen stones and trees, but at one time it seems to
have been much used. To the west the trail bends slightly and disappears from
sight among the pine-trees. To the east the ground slopes down, and the trail
follows the curves of the slope.
name: A Trail through the Forest

desc:
In the middle of the forest clearing stands a huge ant-hill. You see no live
ants around, but the ground is littered with dried ant corpses, which is just as
well, because the ants appear to have been disproportionately large with
savage-looking jaws. Pines stand in circle around the clearing, and the bark of
some of them looks as if it had been chewed on.
name: Clearing in the Dark Forest

desc:
The forest around you is black, but not as if it had been burned out by a fire.
Rather, it looks as if somebody had converted all colours into black and white.
Gone are not only the bright colours of the flowers and berries, gone is also
the green of the leaves and the brown colours of the ground and of the bark. The
trees around you are black and cast black shadows on the parched grey ground.
name: Black Forest

desc:
This clearing in the forest is nothing but a circle of completely burned ground.
Whoever burned it out, had done it more than once and quite thoroughly. No grass
grows here anymore. The trees that once stood here are now nothing but black
stumps, and even the stones on the ground are completely black with soot and
cracked from the heat of fires that raged in this circle.
name: Burned Clearing

desc:
The forest is very dark here, but not because it is very thick. It consists
mostly of broadleaf trees, but the leaves of the trees are very dark, almost
completely black. The undergrowth is scarce here, and also very dark in colour.
The few forest flowers also seem to have lost their bright colours and become
grey, with the faintest tinge of their natural colours.
name: Black Forest

desc:
The forest continues to the north and to the east of you, getting thicker and
darker. A mound of rocks is piled on the ground here. The rocks seem to have
been once heated to such a great temperature that they started to melt, but then
were cooled and became solid again. On the top of the mound there is a flat
black stone with a slight depression in the middle; unlike the rest of the
rocks, it seems to have been unaffected by the heat.
name: Edge of the Forest

desc:
The ground here is covered by a carpet of green moss. A few small mounds rise
from the moss, dotted with red berries; some insects hop around them. Some
patches of the moss are whitened as if they were sprayed with a white paint, and
the berries on these patches are white as if they were unripe. The ground
becomes more rocky to the south. A forest stretches out in all the other
directions.
name: Mossy Clearing

desc:
This is the edge of a sombre pine forest which begins to the west of you. The
vegetation here consists mostly of young pines and fir-trees climbing up the
slightly rising slope to the stone ruins which dominate the top of the hill to
the north. Looking at the ground, you see what seems to be tracks of enormous
wheels, much deeper than any cart-tracks you've ever seen before. No undergrowth
or moss grows on these tracks, and the ground around them seems to be slightly
whitened.
name: Light Pine Forest

desc:
The trees are denser here. Their boles are thicker and branches are strangely
misshapen. To the south there lies a slab of weathered stone, dotted with
patches of dark green moss. Its surface still bears some ancient markings of
unknown significance. There is some stale water in a pair of deeply cut
depressions below the markings.
name: Pine Forest

desc:
A bright spot among the somber pines, a small grassy hill rises here in the
middle of the forest, covered with many small flowers like an elaborate
embroidery. The flowers grow here in patches, some of them white, some yellow,
some blue and some lilac. A few have black borders or black centres as if they
were in mourning. To the east and north the ground slopes up, but to the west
the forest drops into a deep ravine.
name: Flowery Mound

desc:
Pines and firs are the only growing things here. There is no undergrowth or
young trees, because nothing can survive on the barren soil which consists
mostly of rocks, sand and clay. The only sounds in this forest are made by the
chilly wind, which comes here to play from the plains to the north.
name: Light Pine Forest

desc:
A thick carpet of heather covers the ground here. In some spots the heather is
completely withered or discoloured for no visible reason. Obviously, even this
plant, which often grows where other plants would not, does not like the rocky
soil and the harsh weather of this land.
name: Heathery Slope

desc:
The barren plain stretches to the north, its dull grey broken to the northwest
by the glittering grey ribbon of Mitheithel. A few patches of heather grow on
the edge of the plain, but farther to the north even the heather gives up,
leaving the rocky ground completely bare. The realm of Angmar once lay in that
direction, and even though it is now long gone, it is said that its shadow still
lies on the land.
name: Rocky Plains

desc:
The ground slopes up steeply to the east, climbing a rocky plateau. At some
distance to the south a forest of dark pines and firs rises on the slope at the
base of an ancient building. In contrast to the trees of that forest, the few
trees that grow here are crippled birches that seem to be a mockery of their
namesakes from green sunny lands. The tallest birches are about five feet tall
and bent to the ground as if the weight of their branches did not let them stand
straight.
name: Forested Slope

desc:
The steep tree-covered hillside rises from the forest to the top of the hill in
the east. Looking up, you can see the ruins of the wall and an old building,
which looks as if it had once been a temple or a castle. To the south the forest
recedes, allowing you a glimpse of the Old East Road.
name: Forested Slope

desc:
Leafless and barren, the trees stand here as a picture of desolation created by
the northern winds or the winter cold. The dry branches bend to the ground under
their own weight and break with a loud cracking sound. To the south an almost
vertical side of a hill rises before you, its rock surface grey-green with moss
and lichen, seemingly the only living things in this place.
name: Withered Forest

desc:
The large domed hall had obviously once been the entrance hall of the temple,
leading to the altar room and to the inner chambers. The walls and the roof of
the hall still stand intact even though the heavy granite columns that supported
the roof have been broken to pieces which lie on the ground in piles of rubble.
The back part of the hall is completely choked by the debris and does not allow
you even a glimpse of what once lay beyond.
name: The Entrance Hall of the Temple

desc:
A stone building, which had probably once been a castle or a temple, rises to
the north of you. This place seems to have been the courtyard of the building.
In places you can still distinguish the base of the encircling wall, and among
the rubble you can see the remainders of the regular pattern of the stone
pavement. The rest looks as if somebody once lifted this courtyard like a giant
bowl and shook it well till there was no stone left standing and no piece of a
wall left unbroken.
name: Among the Ruins

desc:
Pines grow here in a corner formed by two hill-slopes. The rocky slope to the
north leads to a saddle between two higher points of the ridge. The slope to the
west is much steeper and covered with green moss and climbing pines. It leads to
the top of a hill, on which you can see some ancient ruins. Between the pines on
the western slope there is a somewhat discoloured patch of moss of an irregular
shape.
name: Forest under the Ridge

desc:
A mixed forest of conifers and broadleaf trees grows on the mossy ground. The
foliage and the needles of the trees are as dark as if the sheer cliff that
rises to the north was shutting out not only the northern wind, but also the
sunlight. There is a rock shelf on the side of the cliff about halfway to the
top, and there seems to be a dark opening in the rock wall just above the shelf.
name: Forest under the Ridge

desc:
There is no undergrowth here, and trees stand somewhat apart from each other.
The ground is very dry, as if parched by great heat, and the leaves and bark of
the trees are black or grey, as if the same heat that dried the ground has burnt
them so strongly that they have never been able to recover their natural colour.
Instead of the ordinary smells of the forest, there is a smell here that you
cannot identify - neither the smell of rot, nor the smell of fire, but something
in between. A high ridge rises to the north of you; on all the other sides you
can see nothing but the forest.
name: Black Forest

desc:
A small stream flows through the forest here, its water clear, cool, and
smelling of pine-needles, even though no pines grow in this part of the forest.
Farther from the stream the trees and the undergrowth are black, but trees and
plants on the stream-banks are green, as if the water had healed them or made
immune from whatever drained colour from the rest of the forest. A few patches
of flowers clutter by the stream, their bright colours a sharp contrast to the
blackness of the forest around.
name: Forest Stream

desc:
The forest stream trickles down into a hollow under the roots of trees, feeding
a small pond. The sides and the bottom of the hollow are brown forest soil, but
the water of the pond is so clear that it seems to be shining. You can see every
stone on the bottom of the pond and the few insects and small fishes that hide
in the shadows. The water of the pond smells of earth and pine-needles.
name: Forest Pond

desc:
Trees stand very close to each other in this part of the forest. The air smells
of pine-needles and moist earth. Spider-webs hang among the trees, some of them
as big as four feet in diameter. You notice that for some reason somebody had
cut down a few pines here and let them lie. Following with your eyes the line
formed by the tree-stumps, you see the beginnings of a path.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
The air here is stiflingly damp, and any noise or sound seems to fall heavily
onto the ground, as if it was instantly drenched by the moisture. Many trees
have been uprooted here and arranged into neat piles, obviously prepared for use
as building materials or firewood. Whoever did this must have had inhuman
strength despite quite human reflexes and quite a human desire for a wooden
dwelling or a hot fire.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
The forest soil is soggy and in some places gives under your weight, as if the
forest was in the process of turning into a swamp. A few rotten trees lie on the
ground here blocking the passage to the north. Lichens and moss grow on their
bark, and inside the hollow trunks some insects scurry among the moss.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
Pines grow so densely here, that it is hard to pick the way between their roots
and trunks. The forest soil is brown and moist, and despite the lack of sunlight
every inch of the ground between the boles of the trees is covered with thick
undergrowth, which seems to be fighting for a spot in the rich soil. You can
hear a faint sound of flowing water, but it is not clear from which direction it
is coming. To the north the ground slopes down, and the forest seems to recede.
name: Dark Forest

desc:
A sheer cliff rises out of the forest to the west of you. About six feet from
its base a clear spring flows out from a crevice in the rock face, trickles down
into a bowl carved in the rock and drops from the bowl onto the ground by the
base of the cliff as its journey through the forest begins. There is an air of
majesty and timelessness about the spring as if the power of Ulmo still flowed
in its water.
name: Source of the Stream

desc:
Up on the height the air is cold, and the wind almost blows you off your feet. A
gnarled old pine grows here, bent but undefeated by the wind and the cold. The
roots of the pine hang over a shallow pit, which looks as if stone had once been
quarried there. Lower down you can hear the tree-tops moaning and sighing as the
wind passes through them.
name: On the High Ridge

desc:
The forest is now far below, and the side of the hill is an almost vertical
rock-wall in which there is a stony shelf and a shallow cave, a mere scoop in
the cliff. The wind blows furiously on the height, as if trying to break the
ledge off the wall and send the chunks tumbling into the forest.
name: On a Rock Shelf

desc:
The rocky slope rises from the forested plateau to the north and drops away
sharply just a short distance to the south, forming a narrow saddle between two
higher points of the ridge. To the west a building with a tower rises on the
height. To the east a rocky ledge leads along the southern face of the ridge.
name: On the Ridge of Hills

desc:
The ground here is mostly bare rock with an occasional earth-filled crevice. In
some of these crevices and holes a few hardy pines and firs have managed to
secure their roots. Whether because of lack of nourishment, or because of the
wind and the weather, the trees stand bent in all kinds of weird shapes, as if
undecided in which direction to bend.
name: Edge of the Forest

desc:
A few patches of lichen grow on the rocky ground. To the north of you the
plateau ends with a twenty feet drop to the barren rocky plain below. The way to
the south is blocked by a sheer cliff; in some places the rock of the cliff is
as smooth as if it had been cut by a knife.
name: On the Plateau

desc:
A high ridge of land rises above you, its dark edge against the sky broken into
many bare points like teeth of a blunted saw. The forest has reached its base
and there stopped, as if wondering whether the ascent would be worth the
trouble. Carefully selecting the path among the boulders, one might be able to
climb to the very top of the ridge and enjoy the view of the forest.
name: Under the High Ridge

desc:
The ground rises to the west step by step as if the soil had once slid down here
layer by layer. The slope looks stable now, and the steps are covered with moss
and pines which climb all the way up to the top. To the east the ground becomes
more level and eventually flattens out turning into a rocky plain.
name: Forested Slope

desc:
The rocky plain stretches out to the north and east of you. As far as your eyes
can see, the land to the north is coloured in various shades of grey without a
single green spot. To the west and south the ground slopes up slightly; a forest
of dark pines rises on the slope.
name: Rocky Plain

desc:
The only changing thing in this grey landscape is the sky, to which every
traveller after a while willingly or unwillingly turns his eyes. A rocky plain
might have some kind of personality given to it by the look of its rocks, moss
and lichen, but no watcher would want to look for long at anything with no life
or movement of its own.
name: Rocky Plain

desc:
To the north, a barren rocky plain seems to stretch out to the very horizon, but
if you strain your eyes, you can distinguish at the very edge of sight an uneven
dark line, probably the beginnings of a mountain ridge. The unfriendly North
lies before you, the land with plenty of rock and no inhabitants or vegetation,
the land where all the evil kings have built their citadels until Sauron decided
he preferred warmer lands and company of Men.
name: Rocky Plain

desc:
This used to be the edge of an old wheat field but no one has cultivated this
land for many years. Nearby, to the north in a grove of large trees, lies the
remains of a small number of stone buildings. While only rubbles can be seen
from here, one of these buildings was once quite large and at least two stories
high. To the south the old and abandoned farmland continues for many miles,
though here and there it is broken up by old irrigation channels and crumbled
stone walls.
name: Edge of an Old Wheat Field

desc:
To the north and west the remains of some stone buildings are preceded by a
thick grove of large trees, while the Old East Road north of here slowly winds
its way around the grove and past the buildings. On the other side of the road a
dark and sombre forest covers the hills and an even darker forest swallows up
the old farmland to the south-east.
name: Edge of the Road

desc:
This is the extreme edge of what was once a large cultivated area. North of here
the Old East Road passes, and on its other side a dark and dense forest grows on
top of steep hills. East and south another forest covers the level ground.
name: Edge of the Road

desc:
Oaks, beeches, and some other broadleaf trees you cannot recognise run down the
level slope to the Old East Road to the north. The unknown trees have dark-grey,
almost black, boles and branches. Their dark-green leaves are somewhat like the
oak leaves in shape - except where the edges of oak leaves are round smooth
curves, the edges of these leaves are sharp zigzags, as if these were oak leaves
which decided to grow claws.
name: Edge of the Forest

desc:
This is the edge of a dark forest, which is cut by the Old East Road to the
north and to the east. The trees around are mostly of an unfamiliar kind, with
dark-grey boles and branches and dark-green leaves that resemble a clawed paw.
The foliage of the trees does not seem to be very thick, yet it lets through
almost no light, and no smaller plants seem to survive in the shade.
name: Edge of the Forest

desc:
Old pines loom far above you, blocking out any light the sky might provide. Some
of the withered trees, rotten and ancient, have fallen apart and the splinters
cover the mossy ground. In some places the trees are torn by mortal hands
however, broken and scattered. The forest gives away to a barren plain in the
north. South of here a huge barricade-like pile of old trunks and branches
obstruct any further passage.
name: In the Dark Forest

desc:
Here the trees grow so tall, with crowns so thick, that there isn't a single
place where the sky is visible. The ground is all mud and rocks, and it seems
this part of the forest lacks all signs of animal activity. The smell of pines
and dirt is ever present.
name: Deep inside a Dark Forest

desc:
This part of the forest is old, dark and murky. It smells of decay and half of
the trees are probably dead. Except for the knocked down trees and huge, deep
tracks of trolls there are no signs of living creatures. To the west the forest
lightens some, but to the east and south the forest of Trollshaws stretches
further on.
name: Smothering Grove

desc:
Tall pines with thick, huge crowns stretch towards the skies, preventing both
sunshine and starlight to reach the forest floor. The ground is a strange mix of
mud and moss, dead branches and thorny shrubbery. East and west of here, passage
through the forest is possible.
name: Deep within a Smothering Grove

desc:
The trees here stand so thick that, combined with the multitude of thorny
shrubbery on the ground and climbing the pines, almost any movement is a strain.
There is no way of knowing if the faint glow from far above is the sun or the
moon, or even the stars. It seems as if this old forest wittingly and in a
clever way is blocking all light to reach any of its dwellers. The woods lighten
up just a tad to the south and west. Travel in any direction is made impossible
by the sheer mass of trees, both dead and alive.
name: Smothering Grove

desc:
South and east of here, a dense and dark pine forest grows. The trees are old
and tall, with huge, thick crowns. Here the ground is barren, covered only with
rubble and an occasional sharp, jagged rock. To the west, the plains continue.
name: Rocky Plain

desc:
A still, almost dead-looking, black pool lies here in the midst of the forest.
It smells of decay, and rotten branches and twigs break the surface in numerous
places. To the south the forest looks to grow even thicker, but west of here
passage is still possible without too much strain. The remnants of a wooden sign
lies next to the pond, unreadable from years of decay and erosion.
name: Black and Murky Pool in the Pine Forest

desc:
A small hill, covered by spruce and an occasional young pine, rises above the
forest floor. Even though on higher ground, the dense forest still obstructs
vision in all directions -- the pines standing taller than this hill. A mound
made from smooth, round boulders stands here.
name: Small Hill in the Pine Forest

desc:
Old, tall pines stand here in silence, reaching towards the sky. The ground is
covered by a layer of twigs, pine-needles, moss and dead branches. At some
places miniature spiderwebs hang between the twigs on the ground, and in a few
of the webs an insect or pine-needle has been caught. The forest continues to
the west and south, but to the north it rises somewhat to climb a small hill.
East of here, a flat area lies -- devoid of trees.
name: Dim Pine Forest

desc:
A massive moss-clad boulder stands amidst the numerous pines, shaped almost like
a skull -- the moss being its helmet. Between the trees to the west a forest
trail can be seen. To the east, north and south the dense forest spreads out
undisturbed. Small webs can be seen on some of the bushes, and between twigs on
the ground.
name: Skull-shaped Boulder in a Dreary Pine Forest

desc:
The forest grows dense here, letting only a few rays of sun reach the small
white flowers and twigs on the ground. Some spruce and lots of pine trees grow
here, of which several are very old and thick and a few have reached a great
height, now towering high above the roof of the forest. A trail climbs up from
the south, narrowing some as it turns west.
name: A Trail through the Dense Pine Forest

desc:
The ground slopes slowly to the south, now and then broken by boulders and big
rocks. The forest mainly consists of pine trees, but also spruce can be seen
growing here together with juniper and an occasional thorn bush. Not very broad
but still clear, a trail can be discerned winding its way eastwards through the
twigs and shrubberies under the trees.
name: A Trail in the Forest

desc:
A thick, dense forest of old withered trees and strong pines extends in all
directions. An occasional moss-covered boulder can be found, and on some of the
dead trees some pale fungi are prospering. It seems birds are the only creatures
who inhabit this area, since no signs of any ground-living beasts can be seen.
name: Deep inside a Dense Forest

desc:
Tall, strong pines stand close together here, thick and ever-green. The ground
is covered by a layer of thick, soft, strong-smelling moss. To the west a hedge
can be glimpsed through the trees and underbrush. In all other directions only
forest can be seen.
name: Dense Forest

desc:
North of here, the vast forest stretches undisturbed for miles, but to the south
the trees surrender to the plains. East and west of here the edge of the forest
goes on towards the horizon. Bushes and even an occasional flower cover the
ground here, and there are tracks of small animals everywhere. Tiny spiderwebs
can be seen between the branches of some of the trees.
name: Border of a Pine Forest

desc:
Along the edge of the pine forest to the north, sturdy plants and patches of
flowers can be found, as well as tracks of smaller woodland animals and birds.
South of here the forest gives way to a bushy, flat plain but to the east, west
and north the tall pines rule the land.
name: Border of a Pine Forest

desc:
Thorny bushes and tall pines would make travel in this part of the forest very
taxing, were it not for the worn and wide trail that climbs up to the north and
into the forest to the east. South of here the pines seem to stand a bit further
apart, but in all direction the forest grows as dense and shady as ever.
name: A Trail in the Forest

desc:
Winding its way through the dense forest, dodging boulders and small hedge-like
clumps of shrubbery, the trail is the only reason travel is possible in this
harsh environment. To the west and east, the trail plunges deeper into the
forest. South of here the trees stand tall and silent. Passage north is
impossible because of the severe undergrowth. In some trees tiny spiders have
spun their thin webs.
name: A Trail in the Forest

desc:
The ground being hard-packed earth, covered with a few pine-needles, twigs and a
nut-shell once in a while, this trail makes travel through the old, dense pine
forest less taxing. To the south the forest becomes a tad less dense, but in all
other directions the heavy woods continue. The trail leads on to the west and
east.
name: Trail through a Pine Forest

desc:
East of here, the otherwise dense forest becomes lighter as the number of mighty
pines decreases. A few old, rotting stumps can be seen in some places; like
ancient but still bleeding wounds in the woods. The mossy ground is covered by
twigs and pine cones. North of here a trail leads deeper into the dense
underbrush.
name: Outskirts of a Pine Forest

desc:
Dense bushes and trees shrouded in a multitude of tiny spiderwebs, glimmering in
any light, flank this narrow trail. The pines cease growing to the south, but
the forest maintains its grip on the landscape to the north and west. North of
here however, it seems spruce is dominating the horizon. To the south a large,
flat area lies. Here the trail sits clear, only partially covered with a thin
layer of pine-needles.
name: Trail through a Pine Forest

desc:
A thick layer of brown, dried spruce-needles cover the mossy ground in most
places -- only broken by a few very low plants with blueish green, rounded
leaves. Patches of pale, hard fungi grows on the trunks of some of the tall and
old spruce. To the north the forest opens up to some rocky plains, but to the
east the spruce forest grows as dense as ever. West and south of here the woods
are a bit lighter, mainly consisting of pines.
name: Spruce Forest

desc:
Rotting branches and small twigs cover the ground, in some places covered by
moss and spiderwebs. The pines stand as tall as ever here, and the forest
spreads out in all directions. To the south, a small trail can be glimpsed
through the dense vegetation.
name: Mixed Spruce and Pine Forest

desc:
Two huge pine trees have fallen here, their stumps being charred. Some time ago
this part of the forest must have suffered from a lightning-strike, which
resulted in the fallen trees forming a massive barrier to the west and south.
North and east of here, further passage into the forest is possible.
name: Charred Trees

desc:
East of here a sheer, tall and remarkably flat granite rockface blocks all
passage. Only in a few places is the smooth surface disturbed by erosion or
spidery carvings. A trail stretches through the dense forest just by the
rockface, and it slowly climbs down to the north and south. To the west the
silent forest lies thick and dark.
name: A Trail along a Sheer Rock Face

desc:
The trees grow so dense that passage through this part of the forest is very
hard. Old, withered rocks break the ground in a few places, overgrown by fungi
and covered in dead, brown spruce-needles. To the north a meadow can be
glimpsed, but west of here the spruce forest lies undisturbed. Open fields lie
to the south, and some rolling hills dominate the eastern sky.
name: Spruce Forest

desc:
A huge, mossy meadow dominates this part of the forest. In its middle a tall
mound has been raised, possibly hundreds of years ago. The boulders are somewhat
eroded and covered with fungi and moss. A broken circle of smaller, white, rocks
surround it. The spruce forest stretches onwards to the east and south. To the
north and west a rocky, barren plain lies.
name: A Majestic Mound in a Spruce Forest

desc:
Huge spruce trees grow very close together here, the ground being moist and
mossy. In a few places small rocks show beneath the moss, covered by the cones
and needles which have fallen from the trees. The air is thick, and the forest
lets very little light in -- making this wood very dim. Some small webs spun by
even smaller spiders hang between some branches. To the north and west the
forest strecthes onwards. South of here a large, grass-clad hill stands. To the
east a thorny hedge of brambles and young spruces form an impassable
obstruction.
name: Dreary Spruce Forest

desc:
Here the trees stand close together, forming a massive barrier of sharp needles
and heavy branches. The ground is moist, black earth and very few plants grow
below the spruce. Large spider webs form glimmering spans between the trees,
tiny drops of dew stuck to them reflecting the small and thin rays of light
which passes through the thick woods. To the south one can travel deeper into
the forest. North of here the forest thins as an area of green hillocks begins.
East and west of here, flat plains can be seen -- the eastern one being covered
by tall grass and the western one rocky and barren.
name: Dreary Spruce Forest

desc:
At some places the pines stand so close together there is no way of telling
where the crown on one tree ends and where the next one starts. Dead branches,
splinters and stumps cover the ground, only to be overgrown by brown bushes in a
few places. Travel east from here is made impossible by a very thick hedge,
composed by all sorts of thorn-bushes and young trees.
name: Dense Pine Forest

desc:
The canyon dips to its lowest point here, with sheer rockfaces to the west and
north. East and south of here the canyon continues, sloping upwards to the
surrounding terrain. A few old and broken bones lie on the ground, and in some
places small brown-green plants grows. In the north-west corner of this dip in
the canyon four boulders stand in a square-like formation.
name: Bend in a Narrow Canyon

desc:
Mainly composed by spruce, but also an occasional pine tree, the forest dips
southwards into the plains here. East of here a thick, inpenetrable hedge makes
any passage impossible, but to the west the bush-covered plains stretches on. To
the north the vast, dense and shady forest of pines grows old and ever-silent.
name: Light Forest

desc:
Dead bushes mix with living ones on this seemingly dead plain, which connects
the forest to the north with the slopes to the south. East of here the forest
dips southwards into the plains, but west of here the horizon is clear. To the
south-east jagged, tall hills can be seen.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
Flat plains stretch from the slopes to the south to the pine forest to the
north. West of here a mound can be seen in a distance, but to the east the
plains goes on without any noticable landmarks. Small clumps of bushes and
thorny shrubberies grow here, and even a tiny patch of young spruces has rooted
here.
name: Bushy Plains

desc:
A very wide and tall hill stands here, its western side being a smooth and sheer
granite rockface. A huge circle of mighty pines crown the hill, giving it a
majestic look. East of here smaller hills, overgrown by pines, makes the
landscape look like a forest-green sea of trees, the hilltops being the waves.
To the south flat, rocky plains spread out.
name: Hill Crowned with Pines

desc:
A large open area spreads east- and south-wards. Only rubble and small, ruined
mounds and an occasional bush or patch of slender sillows cover the ground. To
the west a small hill protrudes towards the horizon, it's top just above the
crowns of the pines standing beneath it. North of here a round hill is topped by
a symmetric circle of pines, making it resemble a head wearing a crown of trees.
name: Rocky, Barren Plains

desc:
Rocky ground, devoid of all animal life, spreads out far to the north. The only
signs of any life are the old, abandoned campsites and the broken and withered
bones that can be spotted in a few places. To the south -- ranging through the
south-east, to east of here -- a majestic forest of old and strong-looking
spruce grows. To the west another forest begins, but that one consists mainly of
pines -- them too being tall, old and strong.
name: Rocky, Barren Plains

desc:
Numerous small hills, their sides covered by a large number of tall and
ever-green pines, lie scattered here. To the east the range of hills is covered
by grass, but west of here a huge hill -- crowned with pines -- can be spotted.
South of here a plain lies, its face plagued by the brownish green, thorny
bushes which grow there.
name: Wooded Hills

desc:
The rocky plains lead further on to the west, the ground here broken by tall,
brown-green thorn bushes. A spruce forest can be seen to the south and east, the
powerful trees making the woods look like a green inpenetrable wall. To the
north wooded hills roll smoothly.
name: Thorny Bushes Scattered on Rocky Plains

desc:
Grass-covered hillocks grow into rolling hills to the west, where the trees
dominate the plant-life. Here a large number of different flowers -- mainly
white and light blue -- grow in patches. To the south a forest of mainly spruce,
as well as some willows at the outskirts, grows in silence. The hilly area
surrenders to the grassy plains east of here.
name: Grassy Hillocks

desc:
A quite large, but yet ordinary-looking, hill stands here; green grass and moss
covering its sides. On top of the hill a small spring bursts from the ground,
the water forming a tiny pond. To the south a faint trail can be seen winding
its way over the flat plains. North and west of here a large forest of spruce
trees stand, dark and sinister-looking. To the east some smooth hills can be
seen.
name: Clear Spring on a Hill

desc:
This narrow canyon, windling between the tall and rocky hills, gives no hint of
any plant or animal life. All that can be seen is cracked earth and sheer
rockfaces. The canyon slopes down to the west, and to the east it is possible to
scale a steep hill. To the north the hills disappear as fields begin.
name: Narrow Canyon

desc:
Quite large, blackened boulders lie scattered across the flat lowlands. Brown
and dried grass cover the ground. A narrow canyon begins south of here, digging
its way between the tall hills. The lowlands spread further on east, and west
and north of here a vast pine forest lies.
name: Lowlands

desc:
This large area of flat land spreads further on towards the north, west and
east. The ground is mainly covered by brown, tall grass and some smaller dark
green bushes carrying black-blue berries. To the south a massive of sharp, rocky
hills can be seen.
name: Lowlands

desc:
Brown, dried grass everywhere -- only to be disturbed by the worn earthen trail
which leads west, into the pine forest, and east, onwards through the lowlands.
Row by row, the pointed spruce stabs at the sky north of here, forming a truly
mighty-looking forest. A few hillocks rolls at the eastern horizon.
name: A Trail in the Lowlands

desc:
A narrow trail cleaves the tall grass on the plain covered by tall, brown and
green grass. North of here a large hill stands, the trail leads east into a
hilly area and west further through the grass. South of here the brownish fields
continue. The trail breaks a circle of white rocks on the ground.
name: A Trail in the Lowlands

desc:
A small, dried-out and shallow pond is the only thing which interrupts the
seemingly endless flat lands which spread further on to west and north. Tall,
steep hills pierce the southern horizon, their tops looking a bit like teeth
towards the sky. East of here lies another range of hills, lower and not as
sharp.
name: Lowlands

desc:
The entire area is covered by numerous rather tall hills -- sharp, steep and
jagged. At some places the hillsides are so cracked and rocky that climbing them
would be impossible. A few dried-out bushes stand on one of the hill tops. South
of here stands a very large hill, tall and steep. Looking north, one can see a
huge plain -- only disturbed by an occasional patch of young, slender trees.
name: Steep Hills

desc:
Climbing these hills makes it possible to see for long distances. West of here a
deep and narrow canyon goes to the west, where it after a mile or so bends off
to the south where it disappears behind the hills. To the north the hills get
fewer and fewer as open fields spread out. East and south of here the hills grow
taller, steeper and more jagged. This area is covered by smooth, low hills and
scattered boulders.
name: Rocky Hills

desc:
The cave is a mere scoop in the cliff and provides almost no protection from
wind, rain and snow. Neither does it protect you from the sounds that seem to
come from all directions at once - wind in the chinks of rock, water dripping, a
crack, the sudden rattling fall of a loose stone.
name: Shallow Cave

desc:
The passage, or what remains of it, runs east-west here, ending with an arched
door to the east. Once it had been quite wide and high, with ornamented walls
and curved ceiling, but now the destruction looks as complete as if the hills
themselves finally brought their wrath down on those who dared to disturb their
rest. Somebody has cleaned the rubble just enough to make it possible to pass,
but there is no telling when the walls or the roof might collapse again.
name: Collapsed Passage

desc:
Dark pines and firs climb up the steep slope to the wall of the ancient
half-ruined temple on the hill to the east. A tower of the temple looms almost
precisely above you, and the dark openings of its windows stare into the forest
as if keeping watch. The old stone wall, which is facing you, looks as if it
could collapse under its own weight at any moment, but in the wall there is an
iron door, which has a much fresher look, as if it was recently replaced.
name: Forest by the Temple Wall

desc:
What had once been a storage room of the temple is now used as a guardroom by a
band of orcs. A few torches flare in the brackets on the walls, and in the dim
torchlight you can see the mess of garbage, bones, and rags lying on the floor.
There used to be a door in the eastern wall, but it was broken off the hinges,
and beyond the doorway there is only darkness. An iron door in the western wall
seems to have survived the destruction - maybe because the orcs still have some
use for it?
name: The Guardroom

desc:
The chamber is greater in width than in height and is covered with a low
ornamented dome. There is a circle in the middle of the dome, and many lines
lead from it to the border of the dome like rays from the sun. Two intertwining
stone ribbons or parchments run around the border of the dome; the ends of the
ribbons come down winding on both sides of the arch that opens in the eastern
wall. There once was an iron door in the western wall, but it had been broken
off the hinges.
name: Domed Chamber

desc:
This seems to be the main hall of the temple, the place where the worshippers
gathered to pray and to make sacrifice. A black altar rises in the centre of the
room, and far above it in the domed roof of the hall there is a louver to let
the smoke out. No fire is burning on the altar now, and yet the air seems to be
heavy with its dark smoke. An arch in the southern wall leads into a dimly-lit
hallway. In the back of the room there is a small door, half-hidden in the dark.
name: By the Dark Altar
